coredata.dc:title: Hydroxyl rich graphene quantum dots for the determination of Hg(II) in the presence of large concentration of major interferents and in living cells
coredata.dc:creator: Kappen, Jincymol
Aravind, Manikka Kubendran
Varalakshmi, Perumal
Ashokkumar, Balasubramaniem
John, S. Abraham
coredata.dc:description: Sensitive determination of Hg(II) by photoluminescence (PL) quenching of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been investigated extensively in the past. However, its selective determination was reported only in the presence of same concentration of other interferences. The present study reports the sensitive and selective determination of Hg(II) at pH 7 in the presence of 500- and 1000-fold higher concentration of Fe(III) and other metal ions, respectively using hydroxyl rich GQDs for the first time. The GQDs prepared from citric acid exhibit a strong blue luminescence with excitation independent emission properties. The PL of GQDs was completely vanished after the addition of Hg(II) whereas it was noticeably affected while adding Fe(III). However, the PL remains unaltered while adding other heavy and transition metal ions. Thus, the present fluorescent probe was used for the selective and sensitive determination of Hg(II). As the concentration of Hg(II) increases from 0 to 20 µM, the emission intensity linearly decreases with the correlation coefficient of 0.995 and the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 9.87 nM (S/N=3). Among the different metal ions, Fe(III) alone interferes on the determination of Hg(II). However, the GQDs successfully determine Hg(II) in the presence of 500- and 1000-fold higher concentration of Fe(III) and other metal ions, respectively. The results obtained from the different spectroscopic and microscopic studies indicated that the quenching of GQDs was due to both complexation and formation of metallic Hg. The SEM studies showed that Hg(0) was formed on GQDs surface with size 10-20 nm. Finally, the present fluorescent probe was used to determine Hg(II) in different water samples and lung cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Hg(II) sensor
Selectivity
Complexation
Bioimaging
Real sample analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of GQDs by UV-visible, fluorescence, FT-IR spectroscopy and TEM
Sensitive determination of Hg(II) by spectrofluorimetry
Determination of binding constant and Stern-Volmer quenching constant
Determination of Hg(II) in real samples
Recognition of Hg(II) in living cells
Bioimaging application in lung cancer cell

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A). Emission spectra obtained for each addition of 0.6 µM Hg(II) into GQDs at pH 7.0 (inset: plot of intensity vs. concentration and photographs of (a) GQDs and GQDs containing (b) 4, (c) 8, (d) 12, (e) 16 and 20 µM Hg(II) under UV light). (B) Emission spectra recorded for (a) GQDs and GQDs-50µM Hg(II) with the addition of 1 mM EDTA after incubating the mixture for (b) 0, (c) 5, (d) 10, (e) 15, (f) 20, (g) 25 and (h) 30 min.

(A) Plot of pH vs. change in emission intensity for 0.5 mg/ml GQDs with 20 µM Hg(II). (B) Emission spectra of (a) GQDs containing 10 μM of Mn(II), Co(III), Zn(II), Fe(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), Pb(II) and Cr(III) and 5 μM of Fe(III) and (b-m) after each addition of 10 nM Hg(II) to (a).
Emission spectra obtained for (a) GQDs (1 mg/mL), (b) GQDs with lake water and after the addition of (c) 15 and (d) 25 μM Hg(II) to (b).
(A) Bright field and (B) fluorescence images of A549 cells after incubating with GQDs for 30 min, (C) and (D) after incubating (A) and (B) with 1 µM Hg(II).
Comparison of the present GQDs selectivity towards Hg(II) with the reported CDs and GQDs.

row.entry: 10 nM Hg(II), 10 µM of all interferents and 5 µM Fe(III)

head.ce:title: Hydroxyl rich graphene quantum dots for the determination of Hg(II) in the presence of large concentration of major interferents and in living cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCentre for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, Department of Chemistry, The Gandhigram Rural Institute (Deemed to be University), Gandhigram-624 302, Dindigul, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biotechnology, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Biotechnology, Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive determination of Hg(II) by photoluminescence (PL) quenching of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has been investigated extensively in the past. However, its selective determination was reported only in the presence of same concentration of other interferences. The present study reports the sensitive and selective determination of Hg(II) at pH 7 in the presence of 500- and 1000-fold higher concentration of Fe(III) and other metal ions, respectively using hydroxyl rich GQDs for the first time. The GQDs prepared from citric acid exhibit a strong blue luminescence with excitation independent emission properties. The PL of GQDs was completely vanished after the addition of Hg(II) whereas it was noticeably affected while adding Fe(III). However, the PL remains unaltered while adding other heavy and transition metal ions. Thus, the present fluorescent probe was used for the selective and sensitive determination of Hg(II). As the concentration of Hg(II) increases from 0 to 20 µM, the emission intensity linearly decreases with the correlation coefficient of 0.995 and the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 9.87 nM (S/N=3). Among the different metal ions, Fe(III) alone interferes on the determination of Hg(II). However, the GQDs successfully determine Hg(II) in the presence of 500- and 1000-fold higher concentration of Fe(III) and other metal ions, respectively. The results obtained from the different spectroscopic and microscopic studies indicated that the quenching of GQDs was due to both complexation and formation of metallic Hg. The SEM studies showed that Hg(0) was formed on GQDs surface with size 10-20 nm. Finally, the present fluorescent probe was used to determine Hg(II) in different water samples and lung cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury (Hg(II)) is considered as a hazardous heavy metal ion. Among the three oxidation states, Hg(0), Hg(I) and Hg(II), the toxic nature of later is predominant due to its lipid solubility and bioaccumulation in animal tissues [1]. The Hg(0) released from industries can be easily oxidized by aquatic species and converted into the most toxic form methylmercury (CH3Hg+) which can enter into the food chain and are ingested by human [2]. The accumulation of Hg(II) in the organisms can cause acute health problems such as kidney failure, damage in central nervous system, changes in DNA methylation and affect cardiovascular system [3]. Even trace level of Hg(II) can penetrate through the skin and can seriously affect the animal tissues. Because of the acute poisoning of Hg(II), both WHO and United States Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA) have fixed the limit of Hg(II) in drinking water to 2 ppb [4]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a more sensitive method for the detection of trace level of Hg(II) in water.

The selective sensing of a metal ion in the presence of large concentration of other interfering ions is one of the prerequisites for any kind of a metal ion sensor because the concentration of the interfering ions might be higher than the sensing metal ion concentration in environmental water samples. This is also applicable for a Hg(II) sensor. The detection of Hg(II) in the presence of higher concentration of other interfering ions has been reported by using gold and silver nanoparticles capped with different ligands [5-7]. However, these nanoparticles have few limitations which include high cost and poor stability for long storage. Recently, it has been demonstrated that graphene quantum dots (GQDs) enriched with oxygen functional groups are considered as alternative materials to Ag and AuNPs for the determination of Hg(II) due to their low cost and high stability [8, 9]. The undoped GQDs and hetero atoms doped GQDs have been successfully exploited for the determination of Hg(II) with high sensitivity based on the quenching of GQDs [10-17]. However, the selective determination of Hg(II) was mostly achieved in the same concentration of other interfering ions in the reported papers [13, 18-21]. Further, the previous studies reported that the major interference for the detection of Hg(II) is Fe(III) [22-24]. But, the tolerance limit of Fe(III) and the cause for its interference for the determination of Hg(II) are not addressed [22, 24-26]. Thus, the objective of the present study is to selectively determine Hg(II) in the presence of high concentration of other metal ions including the major interferent, Fe(III) using GQDs synthesized from citric acid (CA). The GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis using CA as a precursor. The blue PL of GQDs was completely disappeared after the addition of 20 µM Hg(II). The results obtained from emission, SEM, TEM and XPS studies confirmed that the GQDs quenching by Hg(II) was attributed to the complexation between Hg(II) and GQDs followed by the formation of Hg(0) on the surface of GQDs. Further, the selective detection of Hg(II) in the presence of 500-fold higher concentration of Fe(III) and 1000-fold higher concentration of other metal ions was achieved using GQDs. Finally, the practical application of the GQDs was showed by determining Hg(II) in different water samples and living cells.

The GQDs were prepared by pyrolysis according to the reported protocol [27] using citric acid (CA) as a precursor with little modification. A detailed preparation procedure was given in the supporting information.

All the PL experiments for the detection of Hg(II) was performed at 25°C. The pH of the GQDs stock solution was 13 and dil. HNO3 was used to adjust the pH to 7. 10 mM of Hg(II) was used as a stock solution.

Water samples were collected from different sources such as bore well, open well and lake near to Hindustan Unilever thermometer factory, Kodaikanal, Tamilnadu, India. Tap water samples were collected from the undergraduate laboratory of Chemistry Department. These samples were used as such for analysis without any pretreatment.

The quantum yield (QY) of as synthesiszed GQDs was determined according to the well known procedure using quinine sulphate as standard. The reported QY for quinine sulphate is 0.54 at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm [28]. Then, QY was calculated according to the equation:
(1)
Q x
=
Q st
(
Gra
d x
/ Gra
d st
)
(
η x
/
η st
)
2
where Q, Grad and η have their usual meaning [28]. Subscript “x” indicates the sample and “st” for standard.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs by UV-visible, fluorescence, FT-IR spectroscopy and TEM
ce:section.ce:para: The presence of oxygen functional groups and fluorescence properties of as synthesized GQDs were characterized by UV-vis, FT-IR and spectrofluorimetry (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2). The obtained results reveal the excitation independent emission of GQDs (Fig. S2A). The TEM image showed that the as synthesized GQDs were roughly spherical in shape (Fig. S2B) and their average size was found to be 1.5 nm (Fig. S2C). The SAED pattern shown in the inset of Fig. S2B indicates that the GQDs were crystalline in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive determination of Hg(II) by spectrofluorimetry
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S3 depicts the emission spectra of 1mg/ml GQDs at pH 7 in the presence of different metal ions. The emission spectrum of GQDs was captured immediately after the addition of 500 µM each Mn(II), Cu(II), Co(III), Ni(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Zn(II), Cr(III), Fe(II), Fe(III) and Hg(II). The emission intensity of GQDs remains unchanged while adding all the metal ions except Fe(III) and Hg(II). The PL intensity was decreased appreciably at 456 nm after the addition of 500 µM Fe(III) to GQDs (curve c) whereas it was completely vanished for the same concentration of Hg(II) (curve b). Since Hg(II) quenches the GQDs PL completely when compared to other metal ions its sensitive determination was carried out by spectrofluorimetry. Fig. 1A depicts the emission titration of 0.5 mg/mL GQDs (pH 7) with different concentrations of Hg(II). During the addition of each 0.6 µM Hg(II), the emission maximum at 456 nm was decreased and completely vanished for 20 µM Hg(II). However, a sequential decrease in emission intensity was not observed and the cause for this behavior will be discussed in the following section. A good linear relationship was obtained while plotting the emission intensity against Hg(II) concentration range of 0--20 µM (R2 = 0.995) (inset of Fig. 1A). The LOD was estimated as 9.87 nM (1.97 ppb) (S/N=3), which is close to the limit prescribed by WHO (2 ppb).

GQDs prepared from CA has been already reported for the sensing of Hg(II) based on GQDs-Hg(II) complexation [25, 26]. In the reported papers, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) or biothiol is used to confirm the complexation between GQDs and Hg(II). For example, GQDs-Hg(II) complex formation was studied by Cong and his coworkers [26]. They reported that the addition of glutathione, cysteine and homocysteine to GQDs-Hg(II) complex resulted the emission recovery. Similarly, emission recovery of GQDs was achieved by Chakraborti et al. with the introduction of EDTA to GQDs-Hg(II) mixture [24]. While adding EDTA to GQDs containing Hg(II), Hg(II) prefers to coordinate with EDTA and this makes the electronic states of GQDs become free. By this way, GQDs were used as “off-on” sensor. The EDTA was used in this study also to break the complexation between GQDs and Hg(II). Fig. S4A shows the effect of EDTA on the fluorescence spectra of GQDs-Hg(II) mixture. The GQDs emission was completely disappeared at 456 nm while adding 50 µM Hg(II) to 1mg/mL GQDs (curves a and b). After a few min, 1 mM EDTA was added to GQDs containing Hg(II). Interestingly, the emission was not completely recovered (curve c). To get more insight into this, EDTA was added immediately to a mixture of GQDs-50µM Hg(II) and then the emission spectrum was recorded (Fig. S4B). Interestingly, it can be seen from Fig. S4B, 90% of GQDs emission was recovered. Further, EDTA was added to GQDs-Hg(II) mixture after each 5 min of incubation to monitor the time dependent emission recovery of GQDs. As depicted in Fig. 1B, the emission recovery with EDTA was decreased gradually after each 5 min of incubation and reached saturation at 25 min and after that no change was noticed. The cause for the unrecovery of GQDs emission after the addition of EDTA and irregular decrease in the emission intensity after the sequential addition of Hg(II) to GQDs (Fig. 1A) is due to the formation of Hg(0) on the surface of GQDs. Very recently, our research group has studied the interaction between Hg(II) and GQDs [29]. Based on the spectroscopic and microscopic studies, it is proposed that first Hg(II) coordinates with GQDs followed by electron transfer from GQDs reduces Hg(II) to Hg(I) and then subsequently Hg(I) reduces to Hg(0). The reduction of Hg(II) by GQDs is schematically represented in Scheme S1.

Further, the above studies reveal that the GQDs PL performance was affected during the formation of Hg(0). As evidenced from Fig. 1A, the PL of GQDs was gradually decreased while increasing the concentration of Hg(II). Since the hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups responsible for the blue luminescence of GQDs were involved in the reduction of Hg(II) to Hg(0), the blue PL was completely disappeared. Further it is evidenced from Fig. 1B, the addition of EDTA also failed to recover the GQDs PL when it was incubated in Hg(II) at different time intervals. This indicates that GQDs PL can not be reverted back after Hg(II) was transformed into Hg(0).

It is obvious that pH can influence the interaction between GQDs and Hg(II) since GQDs contain hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups. In general, GQDs show strong luminescence at an alkaline pH. GQDs emission was drastically quenched at acidic pH because of the aggregation of GQDs [30]. Hence, the pH studies were restricted between pH 13 and 7. Since the pH of the as prepared GQDs is 13, dil.HNO3 was used to adjust the pH. The emission spectra were recorded immediately after adding 20 µM Hg(II) to 0.5 mg/mL GQDs having pH 13, 11, 10, 8, and 7. The change in PL intensity (F0-F) after the addition of Hg(II) was plotted against pH of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 2A, quenching was increased while moving from alkaline to neutral pH under identical experimental conditions. At an alkaline pH, the formation of mercuric hydroxide is more favourable and hence its complexation with carboxylate ion is restricted [31]. On the other hand, when the pH was decreased from 13 to 7, the formation of mercuric hydroxide decreases and therefore Hg(II) can complex with carboxylic acid/carboxylate groups of GQDs along with hydroxyl groups. Thus, effective quenching was observed at pH 7. Since high sensitivity was obtained at pH 7, which is also a physiological pH, pH 7 is chosen for the detection of Hg(II).

Eventhough both undoped and N-doped GQDs and CDs are utilized extensively for Hg(II) sensing, its detection in the presence of high concentration of interefering heavy and transition metal ions is not studied in the reported papers (Table 1 ) [13, 14, 18-20,24, 31-33]. Instead, the selectivity is studied individually by adding either low or equal concentration of metal ions including Hg(II) into GQDs and CDs. But, it is essential to sense Hg(II) selectively in the presence of high concentration of other metal ions because in real sample analysis the presence of other ions with high concentration can not be excluded. In this aspect, the present study aims to sense trace amount of Hg(II) containing high concentration of interfering cations. As shown in Fig. S3, no other metal ions interfere in the determination of Hg(II) except Fe(III). Even though quenching is noticed for GQDs with Fe(III), it was pronounced only at high concentration. Fig. 2B illustrates the emission spectra of GQDs after each 10 nM addition of Hg(II) in the presence of 5 µM Fe(III) and 10 µM of other interfering ions including Mn(II), Co(III), Zn(II), Fe(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Cd(II), Pb(II) and Cr(III). The emission of GQDs remains unchanged for 5 µM Fe(III) and 10 µM of other interfering metal ions (curve a). On the other hand, addition of 10 nM Hg(II) to GQDs containing the aforementioned metal ions, the GQDs emission was decreased (curve b). This reveals that GQDs can determine 10 nM Hg(II) in the presence of 500- and 1000-fold higher concentrations of Fe(III) and other metal ions, respectively. Further, the emission intensity was decreased regularly on each addition of 10 nM Hg(II) (curves c-g). The performance of the present probe towards the determination of Hg(II) with respect to pH, sensitivity and selectivity was compared with the reported GQDs synthesized from CA [22, 29] under different experimental conditions (Table 1). In the reported papers, sensing of Hg(II) was carried out in the presence of same concentration of other interferences [24, 31]. However, Hg(II) was determined in the presence of 500 and 1000-fold higher concentratin of Fe(III) and other interferences, respectively in the present study. Besides, the buffers used in the reported papers (Brittinson-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH 6) [31] and 4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonicacid (HEPES) buffer (pH 7)) [24] are expensive. Further, the advantages of the present fluorescent probe were also compared with N-doped GQDs and CDs (Table 1). It can be seen from Table 1 that the present GQDs showed higher sensitivity and selectivity towards Hg(II) at simple experimental conditions compared to the reported N-GQDs and N-CDs. In addition, Hg(II) was determined in the presence of 500- and 1000-fold higher concentration of Fe(III) and other interfering metal ions in the present study. However, the selective detection of Hg(II) was carried out only in the presence of same concentration of interfering metal ions in the reported papers [18-20,24, 31-33]. Few papers reported higher LOD for Hg(II) [19, 31, 32]. But, it was determined in the presence of same concentration of other metal ions. To sum up, this is the first report for the highly sensitive determination of Hg(II) in the presence of higher concentration of major interferences using GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of binding constant and Stern-Volmer quenching constant
ce:section.ce:para:
The strong interaction between GQDs and Hg(II) was further confirmed by evaluating the binding constant. The binding constant (KA) can be calculated from the following equation [28].
(2)
log
[
F −
F 0
/
F 0
]
= log
K A
+ n
log
[ Q ]

The terms F, F0, KA, n and Q have their usual meaning [28]. The KA was found to be 4.4 × 1013 mol−1 L, by plotting log [F-F0/F0] against log [Q] (Fig. S5A) implying the existance of strong binding between GQDs-Hg(II). Further, Stern-Volmer equation was used to evaluate the quenching constant (KSV) [28].
(3)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K SV

The Stern-Volmer plot of F0/F against [Q] is shown in Fig. S5B. “KSV” is obtained from the slope of Stern-Volmer plot and was found to be 1.09 × 106 mol−1 L. It was observed that there is a positive deviation in the Stern-Volmer plot at higher concentrations of Hg(II). This was attributed to the presence of both dynamic and static quenching [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Hg(II) in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of GQDs for the detection of Hg(II) in real samples was further examined. Fig. 3 illustrates the application of present fluorescent probe in the analysis of Hg(II) in lake water collected from Kodaikanal. The addition of 50 µL of water into 1 mg/mL GQDs did not show any change in the emission intensity (curve b), indicating the absence of Hg(II) in the sample. Hence, known concentration of Hg(II) was spiked into the sample and then emission was recorded. The addition of lake water samples containing 15 and 25 µM Hg(II) to GQDs showed quenching in emission intensity (curves c and d). The obtained results suggest that the present fluorescent GQDs can be used for the determination of Hg(II) in environmental samples. Identical experiments were also done for the water samples collected from chemistry laboratory, bore and open well. The recovery results after spiked Hg(II) to tap, bore and open well water samples containing GQDs are given in Table S1. As shown in Table S1, good recoveries were obtained for water samples collected from different sources. The above results reveal that the GQDs can be succesfully used for the detection of Hg(II) in water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition of Hg(II) in living cells

ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of as synthesized GQDs was analyzed against human lung cancer cell line A549 cell using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay [35]. Initially, A549 cell lines were seeded into a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 h. Further cells were treated with different concentrations of GQDs (20, 40, 60, 80 and 100 µg/mL) for 24 h with incomplete medium. Then, MTT dye was added and incubated for 4 h. Finally, DMSO was added and reading was measured in ELISA reader at an excitation wavelength of 570 nm after 30 min of incubation. The cells that do not expose with GQDs were used as an experimental control. All assays were performed thrice and mean value was represented in Fig. S6. The biocompatibility of GQDs was determined by measuring the half maximum inhibition relative with untreated cells as expressed in percentage. The IC50 value of GQDs towards A549 cell was found to be 51µg/mL. The morphology of the A549 cell incubated with GQDs was appeared as normal as compared to untreated cells. Considering the good biocompatibility of GQDs towards A549 cells, they were used for bioimaging studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging application in lung cancer cell
ce:section.ce:para: The intrinsic toxicity of semiconductor quantum dots is the major disadvantage for the cellular imaging applications. In this context, GQDs giving a good platform towards the cellular imaging because of their low cytotoxicity, photostability and size tuned emission [35]. The cellular imaging studies were carried out by using the as synthesized GQDs on A549 cells. The A549 cells were seed in cell carrier-96 well tissue culture plate and incubated for 24 h. When it reaches the maximum confluence, cells were treated with GQDs for 30 min. Finally, the cells were visualized at an excitation and emission wavelength of 360 and 450 nm, respectively by high content screening to interpret the fluorescent properties of GQDs towards incubated cells. The bright field and fluorescence images of GQDs incubated cells are shown in Fig. 4A and B. The bright field images of GQDs incubated cells showed normal morphology of A549 cells. The blue luminescence of GQDs was retained and is uniformly distributed throughout the cells. This indicated the cell membrane permeability of GQDs which may be due to their size and surface functionalities [36]. Fig. 4C and D show the bright field and fluorescence images of cells together with GQDs treated by 1 µM of Hg(II) for 30 min. The images showed that the fluorescence of GQDs was vanished after interacting with Hg(II). Thus, the present probe is a suitable material for the intracellular determination of Hg(II).

The highly selective and sensitive determination of Hg(II) using GQDs was demonstrated for the first time. The addition of 20 µM Hg(II) was completely quenched whereas the emission was unaffected while adding other heavy and transition metal ions except Fe(III). The complete quenching of GQDs was ascribed to the complexation followed by slow reduction of Hg(II) to metallic mercury. The LOD was found to be 9.87 nM (S/N = 3), which is very close to the limit provided by WHO. Besides sensitivity, the selective determination of Hg(II) was also accomplished using GQDs containing strongly interefering Fe(III). The detection of 10 nM Hg(II) in the presence of 5 µM Fe(III) and 10 µM of other interfering metal ions was achieved. The obtained higher selectivity and sensitivity of the present probe towards Hg(II) was encouraged us to utlize them for its determination in environmental samples. A good recovery results were obtained after spiking Hg(II) into different water samples. Finally, the recognition of Hg(II) in living cells was demonstrated using GQDs.
The financial support from Department of Biotechnology (DBT), New Delhi, India for the (BT/PR20469/BCE/8/1394/2016) is gratefully acknowledged.

Jincymol Kappen: Synthesis and characterization of GQDs, detection of Hg(II) by spectrofluorimetry, original draft preparation; M. K. Aravind: Detection of Hg(II) in living cells; P. Varalakshmi: Editing the biological part; B. Ashokkumar: Methodology for the living cell experiments; S.A. John: Conceptualization and reviewing and editing the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104915.

Endocrinol. Lett.27 (2006) 415-423.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots based nanoprobes for metal ions detection
